games.explanation: Baltimore is getting a home-dog discount that assumes Dean Kremer keeps a good Chicago lineup in check, and Kremer's peripherals have been quietly telling on him all year. Busch and Suzuki are exactly the kind of barrel-heavy bats that turn his mistakes into damage, and a park playing 18% over league on home runs is not the environment where a contact-in-the-zone righty gets to escape clean. The Cubs are also just the better team by a wide margin here, which the market has largely shrugged off. Getting the stronger club at plus money in a launching pad is the entire pitch, and +110 is the receipt.

Grant Holmes has a walk problem that gets worse against right-handed bats, and Pittsburgh happens to run out a lineup full of them who can actually punish free baserunners. When a starter is handing out walks to nearly 13% of the righties he faces, the Pirates don't need to square him up, they just need to be patient enough to get to the soft middle innings. Brandon Lowe and Bryan Reynolds are exactly the barrel-heavy profiles that turn Holmes' 11.6% barrel rate against righties into a scoreboard problem. The market has this as a coin flip and our number says it isn't, so getting the better team at a shade of juice is where the pitch ends.

George Kirby's name still carries ace pricing, but the underlying profile this year is a mid-rotation arm who lets righties square him up. That is a problem in a matchup where Miami rolls out Kyle Stowers and Otto Lopez, both of whom have been menacing right-handed pitching all season, with Stowers in particular sitting on a .395 xwOBA against arms like this. Tyler Phillips is the deserved underdog on paper, but he keeps barrels off the board and the roof-closed environment neutralizes the one thing Seattle's lineup does well. Getting a plus price on the team the numbers actually prefer is how you build a bankroll.

Chase Burns has been a legitimate problem for right-handed hitters and a batting-practice arm for lefties, which is exactly the wrong split to bring into a Phillies lineup that stacks the good side of the plate. Schwarber, Harper, and Marsh all get to sit dead-red against a righty whose numbers against lefty bats balloon to a .323 xwOBA, and Burns has walked nearly 12% of the lefties he's faced. Add in a Cincinnati park that turns fly balls into souvenirs and you have the ideal environment for a Schwarber swing to decide things by the fifth inning. Philadelphia is the better team, the better matchup, and somehow still plus money; that is the entire pitch.

picks.explanation: Baltimore is getting a home-dog discount that assumes Dean Kremer keeps a good Chicago lineup in check, and Kremer's peripherals have been quietly telling on him all year. Busch and Suzuki are exactly the kind of barrel-heavy bats that turn his mistakes into damage, and a park playing 18% over league on home runs is not the environment where a contact-in-the-zone righty gets to escape clean. The Cubs are also just the better team by a wide margin here, which the market has largely shrugged off. Getting the stronger club at plus money in a launching pad is the entire pitch, and +110 is the receipt.

Detroit is the better team, nobody's disputing that, but a run and a half of insurance against a rookie starter who has faced the league exactly 217 times is a different conversation than the moneyline suggests. Troy Melton has been quietly hittable against left-handed bats, and the A's happen to run out Nick Kurtz, whose numbers against righties (.423 xwOBA) read like a printing error. Springs isn't an ace, but he limits hard contact well enough to keep this from turning into the 8-3 laugher the market is quietly pricing in. A projected half-run margin doesn't cash the moneyline, but it cashes this line comfortably, and -145 for a run and a half in a game the sim sees as a coin flip is the entire argument.
